ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper's main novelty is empirical: creation and UD-style annotation of a Singlish dependency treebank (1,200 sentences) and analysis/adaptation of UD guidelines for Singlish-specific phenomena (topic-prominence, copula/NP deletion, discourse particles). The modeling contribution largely applies existing methods (bi-affine Dozat & Manning parser; Chen et al. neural stacking for POS and parser; GloVe embeddings) to this new language, yielding useful transfer results but not fundamentally new algorithms. Thus the work is a moderately novel, application-driven contribution (new resource + adaptation/analysis) rather than a methodological advance.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important real-world gap—dependency parsing for the low-resource creole Singlish—by creating a Singlish dependency treebank (1,200 sentences) and showing effective transfer from English via neural stacking. Empirically it yields substantial gains (36.5% relative error reduction; UAS 84.21%) and large POS-tagging improvements, demonstrating a practical method for adapting state-of-the-art parsers to dialects/creoles. Releasing the treebank and parser should enable downstream work (information extraction, sentiment) and encourage similar transfer approaches for other low-resource varieties, giving the work clear and reasonably broad impact.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally organized into standard sections (Related Work, Treebank, POS tagging, Parsing, Experiments) and includes useful figures/tables (e.g. Figure 2 examples, Table 2 results, Figures 3–6 architectures) that help comprehension. However the provided text contains many formatting artifacts and duplications (interleaved line numbers 301–399, repeated paragraphs, LaTeX/footnote remnants and cut sentences) that make some passages hard to follow and interrupt the logical flow. Notation and acronyms are mostly consistent (UAS/LAS, bi-LSTM-CRF, neural stacking) but a few terms are not introduced explicitly early, and occasional grammatical/formatting issues reduce clarity. With clean formatting and removal of duplicated fragments the paper would be well written and easy to read.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper is squarely within top-tier NLP scope: it builds a new Universal Dependencies Singlish treebank (1,200 sentences) and addresses dependency parsing for a low-resource creole—topics of clear interest to ACL/EMNLP. It targets the right audience by combining resource creation with transfer-learning methods (neural stacking from Chen et al. 2016 and biaffine parser of Dozat & Manning 2016) and evaluates with standard parsing metrics (UAS/LAS) and error analysis (reports ~36% relative error reduction, UAS ≈84.2%). The problem formulation, use of state-of-the-art methods, and positioning with related work on multilingual/transfer parsing make it an excellent fit for a top NLP venue.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares the proposed neural-stacking approach against sensible baselines: an English-trained parser (UD-Eng), a Singlish-only baseline, and ablations with different embeddings (Tables 2 and reported UAS/LAS, Section 6.3). Comparisons use the same data and metrics and include error analyses by grammar type and POS-tagging ablation, showing fair and informative evaluation. However, it does not provide quantitative head-to-head experiments against other recent cross-lingual/multilingual transfer methods (e.g., Ammar et al., Guo et al.) despite citing them, so a relevant class of competing approaches is omitted.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper presents a solid set of experiments supporting its claims: POS and parsing improvements from neural stacking are quantified (POS: Stack-ICE-SIN 84.79% vs Base-ICE-SIN 77.77%, parser UAS best 84.21% with 36.5% relative error reduction), multiple embedding sources are compared, and an error analysis across Singlish grammar types is reported. There are some sensitivity checks (choice of stacked bi-LSTM size) and comparisons to English vs Singlish-trained models, but deeper ablations of the stacking components (e.g. which transferred features matter most), statistical significance, and more extensive failure-case analysis are limited. Related work is adequately covered and training/configuration details (hyperparameters, pretraining sources, jackknifing) are provided to enable reproduction, though a few implementation/ablation specifics are omitted.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims (constructing a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank and improving a Singlish parser by neural-stacking English models) are clearly stated and supported by experiments that compare reasonable baselines (English-on-Singlish, Singlish-only with ICE-SIN embeddings) and show a best UAS of 84.21% (reported 36.51% relative error reduction). There are no formal mathematical derivations to check and the use of established architectures (biLSTM-CRF for POS, Dozat & Manning biaffine parser, Chen et al. neural stacking) is appropriate; the authors also performed jackknifing for POS training and a targeted error analysis by grammar type. Minor concerns remain: the paper omits some experimental details (explicit train/dev/test splits for parsing, whether English components were frozen or fine-tuned during stacking) and reports no statistical significance testing, which limits reproducibility and the strength of empirical claims but does not invalidate the main technical conclusions.
